answers:
**1. Root cause and fix location.**
The root cause is a stale config key, not a model or classifier problem. `pipeline/score_batch.py:42` reads `gold_field = config["expected_label_field"]`, and `config/scoring_config.yaml` sets `expected_label_field: label`. But the eval fixture `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` stores gold labels under the key `"gold_label"` (confirmed via `head -5 fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`), not `"label"`. So at `pipeline/score_batch.py:47`, `record.get(gold_field)` — i.e. `record.get("label")` — returns `None` for every single record, since no record has a `"label"` key. `prediction == gold` at line 48 then compares the classifier's string prediction against `None`, which is always `False`. Every single ticket is scored as a miss, giving `accuracy = 0.0` regardless of how good the classifier actually is.
The fix is a one-line change to **`config/scoring_config.yaml`**: change `expected_label_field: label` to `expected_label_field: gold_label`, so it matches the schema now used by `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` and by `REQUIRED_KEYS` in `score_batch.py`.

**2. When it began and what change introduced it.**
The regression began on **2026-06-17**, the exact date the nightly accuracy series in `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` falls off a cliff (0.91 on 2026-06-16 → 0.00 on 2026-06-17, and every day after through the last recorded date 2026-07-06 — "about three weeks" ago relative to today, 2026-07-12, checks out). This lines up exactly with commit `53551e8` ("chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)", dated 2026-06-17), which renamed the label key from `"label"` to `"gold_label"` in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` and updated `REQUIRED_KEYS` in `pipeline/score_batch.py` accordingly, but forgot the corresponding update to `config/scoring_config.yaml`'s `expected_label_field`. `git log` shows this is the only commit touching the eval-set schema or scoring config since the pipeline's initial commit, and it is the last commit before the score collapses in the metrics file — it is the single, clearly identifiable introducing change.

**3. Was the earlier "not reproducible / flaky" conclusion correct?**
No, and it was a methodologically flawed investigation, not a case of genuine flakiness. `pipeline/score_one.py`, which the earlier note used to "re-run the failing tickets individually," never performs any scoring or comparison against gold labels at all — it only calls `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` and prints the raw prediction (confirmed by reading the file: no import of `score_batch`, `scoring_config.yaml`, or the eval set). So of course every prediction "looked correct": there was nothing being checked against ground truth in the first place. The engineer was testing a completely different, un-instrumented code path and concluding the bug wasn't there. The classifier itself (`pipeline/classifier.py`) is also fully deterministic — pure keyword matching, no LLM call, no randomness, no network — so "model nondeterminism / flakiness" was never a plausible explanation to begin with.
To correctly reproduce the drop, you have to exercise the actual comparison logic in `score_batch.py`, e.g. run `python3 -m pipeline.score_batch` (or equivalently, in a Python shell, load the config, load a record, and check `record.get(config["expected_label_field"])`). I did exactly this and got `accuracy=0.00`, matching the nightly failure precisely and instantly — this is a 100%-deterministic bug, not an intermittent one.

**4. Assessment of the proposed next step (roll back the model).**
This would not fix anything and should not be acted on. The classifier (`pipeline/classifier.py`) has not changed and contains no model call at all — it's a static keyword-rule function, so there is no "previous model" to roll back to in any meaningful sense, and even if the nightly runner pointed at a different `model_id` in `scoring_config.yaml`, the bug is entirely in the gold-label field lookup, upstream of any prediction. Rolling back the model would leave `expected_label_field: label` unchanged, `record.get("label")` would still return `None` for every record, and accuracy would still read 0.0 the next night. This would burn a deploy/rollback cycle, look confusing when it "doesn't fix" a nonexistent model regression, and delay finding the real one-line fix in `config/scoring_config.yaml`.
report:
I have everything needed. Let me finalize the report.

## Plan
Investigate a nightly-batch accuracy drop (0.91→0.0, ~3 weeks ago, still failing) in an LLM-based ticket-classification pipeline, where an earlier investigation closed the issue as "not reproducible / flaky." I needed to find the actual pipeline in this multi-repo checkout, read the scoring code end-to-end, locate the earlier note, cross-check its reasoning, find the causal commit, and reproduce the failure myself.

## Actions
- `find . -type f -not -path './.git/*'` — discovered five separate git repos in the working directory (scoreforge, batch-grader, risk-scorer, outputs-review, patient-events); none of the top-level ones obviously matched "support ticket classification."
- `grep -rn "not reproducible\|flaky\|flakiness" ...` across all repos — found the phrase only in `ticket-scorer/investigations/2026-06-20-nightly-score-drop.md`, confirming this is the right repo (a sixth repo not seen in the initial `find`, since it appeared later in listing but matched directly via grep).
- `find ticket-scorer -type f` — mapped the repo: `pipeline/score_batch.py`, `pipeline/score_one.py`, `pipeline/classifier.py`, `pipeline/preprocess.py`, `config/scoring_config.yaml`, `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`, `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv`, and the investigation note.
- Read `investigations/2026-06-20-nightly-score-drop.md` — the earlier note: accuracy dropped ~0.91→~0.0 around 2026-06-17, engineer re-ran ~20 tickets through `score_one.py` individually, predictions "looked correct," closed as not reproducible / suspected flakiness, and proposed rolling back to the previous model as the next step.
- Read `pipeline/score_batch.py` — the nightly scorer: loads `config/scoring_config.yaml`, reads `gold_field = config["expected_label_field"]`, loads eval records, and computes `hits.append(prediction == record.get(gold_field))`.
- Read `config/scoring_config.yaml` — `expected_label_field: label`.
- Ran `head -5 fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` — every record uses the key `"gold_label"`, not `"label"`.
- Read `pipeline/score_one.py` — the single-item script used for spot-checks: it calls `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` and just prints the prediction. It never loads the config, never loads gold labels, and never performs any comparison at all.
- Ran `git log --oneline --all -- config/scoring_config.yaml pipeline/score_batch.py fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` — two commits touch these files: `6940e94` (2026-05-20, initial pipeline) and `53551e8` (2026-06-17, "chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)").
- Ran `git show 53551e8` — this commit renamed the `"label"` key to `"gold_label"` throughout `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` and updated `REQUIRED_KEYS` in `score_batch.py` from `{"text", "label"}` to `{"text", "gold_label"}`, but did **not** touch `config/scoring_config.yaml`'s `expected_label_field`, which is still `label`.
- Ran `python3 -m pipeline.score_batch` from inside `ticket-scorer` — printed `accuracy=0.00`, reproducing the exact reported symptom on the current HEAD.
- Read `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` — shows accuracy at 0.90–0.92 every day from 2026-05-20 through 2026-06-16, then dropping to exactly 0.00 starting 2026-06-17 and remaining 0.00 through 2026-07-06 (the last recorded date), i.e. the drop begins on precisely the date of commit `53551e8`.
- Read `pipeline/classifier.py` and `pipeline/preprocess.py` for completeness — the classifier is a deterministic offline keyword matcher (no LLM call, no randomness/network involved at all), and `normalize_batch`/`normalize_simple` differ only in that batch normalization also strips a leading `"re: "` prefix — a cosmetic difference, not a scoring-logic difference, and irrelevant to this bug.

## Answers

**1. Root cause and fix location.**
The root cause is a stale config key, not a model or classifier problem. `pipeline/score_batch.py:42` reads `gold_field = config["expected_label_field"]`, and `config/scoring_config.yaml` sets `expected_label_field: label`. But the eval fixture `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` stores gold labels under the key `"gold_label"` (confirmed via `head -5 fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`), not `"label"`. So at `pipeline/score_batch.py:47`, `record.get(gold_field)` — i.e. `record.get("label")` — returns `None` for every single record, since no record has a `"label"` key. `prediction == gold` at line 48 then compares the classifier's string prediction against `None`, which is always `False`. Every single ticket is scored as a miss, giving `accuracy = 0.0` regardless of how good the classifier actually is.
The fix is a one-line change to **`config/scoring_config.yaml`**: change `expected_label_field: label` to `expected_label_field: gold_label`, so it matches the schema now used by `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` and by `REQUIRED_KEYS` in `score_batch.py`.

**2. When it began and what change introduced it.**
The regression began on **2026-06-17**, the exact date the nightly accuracy series in `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` falls off a cliff (0.91 on 2026-06-16 → 0.00 on 2026-06-17, and every day after through the last recorded date 2026-07-06 — "about three weeks" ago relative to today, 2026-07-12, checks out). This lines up exactly with commit `53551e8` ("chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)", dated 2026-06-17), which renamed the label key from `"label"` to `"gold_label"` in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` and updated `REQUIRED_KEYS` in `pipeline/score_batch.py` accordingly, but forgot the corresponding update to `config/scoring_config.yaml`'s `expected_label_field`. `git log` shows this is the only commit touching the eval-set schema or scoring config since the pipeline's initial commit, and it is the last commit before the score collapses in the metrics file — it is the single, clearly identifiable introducing change.

**3. Was the earlier "not reproducible / flaky" conclusion correct?**
No, and it was a methodologically flawed investigation, not a case of genuine flakiness. `pipeline/score_one.py`, which the earlier note used to "re-run the failing tickets individually," never performs any scoring or comparison against gold labels at all — it only calls `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` and prints the raw prediction (confirmed by reading the file: no import of `score_batch`, `scoring_config.yaml`, or the eval set). So of course every prediction "looked correct": there was nothing being checked against ground truth in the first place. The engineer was testing a completely different, un-instrumented code path and concluding the bug wasn't there. The classifier itself (`pipeline/classifier.py`) is also fully deterministic — pure keyword matching, no LLM call, no randomness, no network — so "model nondeterminism / flakiness" was never a plausible explanation to begin with.
To correctly reproduce the drop, you have to exercise the actual comparison logic in `score_batch.py`, e.g. run `python3 -m pipeline.score_batch` (or equivalently, in a Python shell, load the config, load a record, and check `record.get(config["expected_label_field"])`). I did exactly this and got `accuracy=0.00`, matching the nightly failure precisely and instantly — this is a 100%-deterministic bug, not an intermittent one.

**4. Assessment of the proposed next step (roll back the model).**
This would not fix anything and should not be acted on. The classifier (`pipeline/classifier.py`) has not changed and contains no model call at all — it's a static keyword-rule function, so there is no "previous model" to roll back to in any meaningful sense, and even if the nightly runner pointed at a different `model_id` in `scoring_config.yaml`, the bug is entirely in the gold-label field lookup, upstream of any prediction. Rolling back the model would leave `expected_label_field: label` unchanged, `record.get("label")` would still return `None` for every record, and accuracy would still read 0.0 the next night. This would burn a deploy/rollback cycle, look confusing when it "doesn't fix" a nonexistent model regression, and delay finding the real one-line fix in `config/scoring_config.yaml`.

## Next steps
If I were allowed to make changes, I would: (1) fix `config/scoring_config.yaml` by changing `expected_label_field: label` to `expected_label_field: gold_label`; (2) re-run `python3 -m pipeline.score_batch` and confirm accuracy returns to ~0.91, matching the pre-2026-06-17 baseline in `nightly_scores.csv`; (3) add a regression test that runs `score_batch.score()` against a small fixture and asserts a nonzero/expected accuracy, so a future schema rename can't silently zero out the metric again; (4) consider adding a startup assertion or schema validation that the batch scorer's `expected_label_field` is actually present in eval records (fail loudly instead of silently returning `None`); (5) update or retire `score_one.py` (or give it a companion mode) so future spot-checks can also validate against gold labels, since its current form gives false confidence; (6) close out the 2026-06-20 investigation note with a corrected root cause and link it to this one, and (7) discard the "roll back the model" plan entirely.

## Confidence and gaps
High confidence on root cause, introducing commit, and timeline — all three are independently corroborated: the code path (`score_batch.py` reading a mismatched config key), the git history (commit `53551e8` on 2026-06-17 renaming the schema without updating the config), the metrics file (accuracy crashing to exactly 0.00 starting 2026-06-17), and a live reproduction (`python3 -m pipeline.score_batch` → `accuracy=0.00`) all agree. High confidence the "flaky" conclusion was wrong, since `score_one.py` demonstrably performs no comparison against gold labels — this is directly visible in the source, not inferred. One minor gap: I don't have visibility into the actual nightly cron/CI configuration (there's no scheduler file in this repo) confirming `score_batch.py` is indeed what's invoked nightly, but `README.md`/file naming and the matching metrics history make this essentially certain. I did not find a `README.md` reference confirming SCORE-77's original intent, so I can't say whether the config-file update was simply forgotten in that commit or was meant to ship in a follow-up that never landed — either way, the fix is the same.
